topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet afternoon. A name flashed: Mark. Mark, the architect of scraped knees and whispered cruelties in the third grade. My thumb hovered over the 'Accept' button, a cold knot tightening in my chest. It felt like reaching for a live wire. I stared at the profile picture—a forced, overly confident smirk—and the air in my small apartment seemed to thin out. Every muscle in my jaw clenched. I didn't move. Just the slow, heavy rhythm of my breathing, shallow and uneven, echoed in the silence. It was a physical weight, this sudden reappearance, pressing down on my ribs until I felt breathless. I finally closed the app, the screen going dark, leaving behind a residue of bitter ash.

[story 2]

I reread the message three times. It was short, almost aggressively casual: "Hey, long time no see. Thought we should catch up." A strange, hollow ache settled deep in my stomach, a familiar, nauseating churn. I traced the outline of the text on the glass, my fingers trembling slightly. Looking at the request felt like reopening a wound I thought had long since scabbed over. My hands started to shake more noticeably, and I instinctively pulled my arms across my body, trying to create a barrier between myself and the phantom memory of that small, cruel boy. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room as if trying to outrun an invisible tide. The floorboards beneath my bare feet felt strangely distant, and a tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

The chime sounded, sharp and unwelcome. I didn't look immediately; I just stared at the phone resting on the bedside table, the light reflecting off the screen like a harsh interrogation lamp. When I finally glanced down, the name swam into focus: Alex. The sensation that washed over me wasn't sadness, not exactly; it was a profound emptiness, like a room suddenly stripped bare of all its furniture. My gaze drifted to the window, where the city lights blurred into streaks of meaningless color. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the incoming connection. A slow, involuntary tremor ran through my hands, making them shake against the cool wood of the nightstand. I didn't know what I was supposed to feel, only that everything felt profoundly unbalanced, tilted off its axis.
